_meta.description: Golden evaluation set for the qwen-rag pipeline. Every expected fact below is verbatim-grounded in eval/corpus/. Do not add a question whose answer is not literally in the corpus.

categories.easy_factual: Single fact, stated plainly in one place.
categories.precise_lookup: Exact tokens: error codes, numbers, acronyms, proper nouns, file paths. These are where dense-only retrieval is weakest.
categories.multi_hop: Answer requires combining facts from two or more separate sections.
categories.follow_up: Conversational. The question is meaningless without chat_history — tests whether retrieval uses conversation context.
categories.distractor: Answer lives in the OTHER document. Tests that retrieval routes to the right source.
categories.unanswerable: Answer is NOT in the corpus. The system must abstain. Tests hallucination resistance.

fields.key_facts: Literal strings that a correct answer / correct retrieved context must contain. Matched case-insensitively.
fields.expected_source_docs: Which document(s) the evidence must come from. Empty for unanswerable.
fields.chat_history: Prior turns, for follow_up questions only.

questions.query: What is FundForge?
questions.expected_answer: A full-stack crowdfunding platform where creators launch campaigns, donors donate, and admins moderate.

questions.query: Which database does FundForge use?
questions.expected_answer: PostgreSQL, accessed exclusively through the Prisma ORM.

questions.query: What are the three user roles in the system?
questions.expected_answer: ADMIN, CREATOR, and DONOR.

questions.query: How are passwords stored?
questions.expected_answer: Hashed with bcrypt, never stored in plaintext.

questions.query: What build tool does the frontend use?
questions.expected_answer: Vite, which provides native ES modules and hot module replacement in development.

questions.query: Which library is used to render charts on the dashboard?
questions.expected_answer: Recharts, an SVG-based chart library built for React.

questions.query: What HTTP status code is returned when a user is logged in but has the wrong role?
questions.expected_answer: 403 Forbidden.

questions.query: What status code is returned when someone registers with an email that already exists?
questions.expected_answer: 409 Conflict.

questions.query: Which Prisma error code maps to a 409 Conflict response?
questions.expected_answer: P2002, the unique constraint violation error.

questions.query: Which Prisma error code maps to a 404 Not Found?
What status code does the API return when the rate limit is exceeded?
questions.expected_answer: 429 Too Many Requests.

questions.query: How many bcrypt salt rounds does the backend use?
questions.expected_answer: 12 rounds, configured via BCRYPT_SALT_ROUNDS.

questions.query: What is the exact rate limit applied to the API routes?
questions.expected_answer: 100 requests per 15 minutes per IP address.

questions.query: How long is the access token valid, and how long is the refresh token valid?
questions.expected_answer: Access token: 15 minutes. Refresh token: 7 days.

questions.query: What does RBAC stand for?
questions.expected_answer: Role-Based Access Control.

questions.query: What does DTO stand for and what is it used for here?
questions.expected_answer: Data Transfer Object — defines the shape of data crossing the API boundary; implemented as Zod schemas.

questions.query: What does ORM stand for?
questions.expected_answer: Object-Relational Mapper.

questions.query: What base URL does the frontend Axios instance point at?
questions.expected_answer: http://localhost:4000/api/v1

questions.query: What is the maximum page size allowed on paginated list endpoints?
questions.expected_answer: 100 — it prevents 'give me all 10,000 records' attacks.

questions.query: Which file exports the Prisma client singleton?
questions.expected_answer: backend/src/config/database.ts

questions.query: What is the Axios request timeout?
questions.expected_answer: 15 seconds.

questions.query: An access token expires mid-session. Walk me through exactly what happens so the user never sees a login screen.
questions.expected_answer: The API returns 401. The Axios response interceptor catches it, calls POST /auth/refresh with the refresh token. The backend verifies the refresh token's signature and checks the DB, then revokes the old refresh token and issues a new one (rotation). Axios saves the new tokens and retries the original request. The user notices nothing.

questions.query: A user has the CREATOR role and tries to edit a campaign belonging to a different creator. What happens, and why isn't the role by itself enough?
questions.expected_answer: Having the CREATOR role is not sufficient — an ownership check in the service layer verifies creatorId matches the requesting userId. If the user is neither the owner nor an ADMIN, it throws ApiError.forbidden(), producing a 403.

questions.query: Why must creating a donation run inside a database transaction, and which records get written or updated?
questions.expected_answer: Because it touches multiple tables and a partial failure would corrupt data. In one transaction: create donation, create payment, update campaign raisedAmount, update campaign donationsCount, update donor totalDonated, update reward claimsCount. Either all succeed or none do.

questions.query: Someone steals a refresh token and tries to use it after the real user already used it. What does the system do?
questions.expected_answer: Refresh token rotation means each token is invalidated on use. Reuse of an already-used token is detected as token reuse, and the system revokes ALL of that user's tokens, forcing a full re-login.

questions.query: What should NOT be stored in it?

chat_history.content: What is Zustand used for in this project?

Zustand is the client state management library. It holds global auth state — the current user object, the access token, the refresh token, and an isAuthenticated boolean — so any component can read it.

questions.expected_answer: Server data (campaigns, donations) must not go in Zustand — that is React Query's job. Component-local state (like whether a modal is open) also must not — that is useState's job.

questions.query: Which of those layers is the only one allowed to touch the database?

chat_history.content: Explain the layered architecture on the backend.

There is a strict 4-layer architecture: Routes map HTTP verbs to controllers, Controllers handle HTTP concerns only, Services hold business logic, and Repositories handle data access. Each layer talks only to the one directly below it.

questions.expected_answer: Layer 4, the Repositories. Database queries must never appear outside a repository — writing prisma.something in a service is explicitly banned.

questions.query: What is it built with?

chat_history.content: Which library renders the dashboard charts?
Recharts — a chart library made for React, used on the dashboard analytics pages.

questions.expected_answer: Recharts is built with SVG.

questions.query: What happens when you exceed it?

chat_history.content: Does the API have a rate limiter?
Yes — express-rate-limit caps requests per IP within a time window to prevent brute-force and abuse.

questions.expected_answer: Exceeding the rate limit returns 429 Too Many Requests.

questions.query: What height did the Lunaria Nova plant reach?
questions.expected_answer: 23 cm.

questions.query: What kind of music makes the plant stop growing, and how fast?
questions.expected_answer: Heavy metal music — it stops growing within 48 hours.

questions.query: What is FundForge's Kubernetes deployment strategy and which cloud provider hosts it?
questions.expected_answer: ABSTAIN — the corpus says nothing about Kubernetes, deployment, or hosting.

questions.query: How much does a FundForge premium subscription cost per month?
questions.expected_answer: ABSTAIN — the corpus contains no pricing information.
